sentences.text: My name is Rose Kohn, and-- K-O-H-N.
My maiden name is Eizman, E-I-Z-M-A-N.
I was born in Poland, Lodz, in 1924-- October 10th.
My mother and father-- we were a loving family, but kind of a hard-working, hard-working family.
And my father worked for 16, 16 hours a day.
At that time, they did not have a union.
Shortly before the war, came-- a union came in.
So she-- he only worked for eight hours.
But 16 hours a day, hard-working man.
He was, like, a machinist for stockings, stockings.
The, the machines were making stockings.
And he fixed-- he was fixing those machines--
And, and he didn't make much.
But somehow, we were not hungry.
And-- and I had a sister, one sister.
She was three years older than I.
And we also had a grandma, my mother's mother, that lived with us the last few years-- very ill.
And we all were in one little room--
She was a-- she worked at home.
She was a seamstress, sewing children's clothes.
And, and well, but it was-- we were happy.
We were a happy, happy family.
My father, my parents, were Bundists.
So there was not really-- we didn't have a religious-- very religious home.
And they believed wherever they live, this should be their home.
They did not think that they have to go to Israel-- Eretz Yisrael, Zionism.
They just wanted peace and live in the countries they live in--
--and live their life as good people, I guess.
No, not politically active, just active in this organization.
This is far as I know.
My father's name was Lajzer Eisman.
My mother's was Mary Malka Eisman.
Her mother's maiden name-- her mother's maiden name was Danziger.
It was a big, big building.
Well, it was a big building.
On one side-- one side, wealthy people lived, with-- you know, with nice incomes.
On the other side, very poor-- some had nothing.
And that was a big yard that the kids could play.
I, I, I found it comfortable then--
--you know, [LAUGHS] I wouldn't find it now, probably.
Only the janitor was not Jewish.
Well, because we were just separated.
Jews-- not by law, but Jews separated.
I mean, they had their own-- there were Polish people, too.
Next to me, next to our building, was a, a elementary school only for Polish--
And I went to a school about a mile away or maybe two miles away, where only Jewish people--
And then-- was a church not far from me, you know.
And let me tell you, fear was there.
I remember being very fearful, very, very fearful.
First, I mean, all my life I would hear, Jew, Jew, Jew.
I mean, there, there was always the Jew.
When kids were coming home-- going home from school when school was over, if I would meet them out in the street-- and they would say, Jew to Palestine, you know.
And, and shortly before the war, maybe two years, three years, there was a big movement of anti-Semitism in Poland.
And one of the quotas was on our street where I lived.
And not the-- you know, they, they would meet.
So after a meeting, everybody was frightened.
And my mother was so frightened about my father because he had to walk home from work or go to work, that I grew up with lots of fear, lots of fear that something bad can happen--
And I think from childhood on, I was conditioned, being a Jew.
Sometimes I feel that it was easier, maybe, for me-- [CRYING] pardon me--
[CRYING] --to survive [CRYING] the camp and knowing that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they're against us.
Because it wasn't a shock to me.
I knew didn't do anything wrong.
I knew we were wonderful people, that the others were wrong--
But I feel, sometimes-- I could be wrong-- that it was a little easier for me to go through this horrible--
I was conditioned from childhood on-- against the Jew, against the Jew.
But she was-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] because a few people were killed, you know, that-- after, after their meetings.
There was so much hate that they were coming out, and they, they killed a, a, a baker.
They had a gun and, and shot him, yeah, uh-huh.
And he was just not doing anything, just--
Only because he was a Jew, yeah.
So there was a lot of anti-Semitism in Poland.
No, they just-- there, there was quite a movement of that Bund in, in, in Poland.
It was a big Jewish community.
Yeah, big Jewish community, right, right.
And we all tend to live together.
A few people lived outside of this-- that area.
There were some Jewish people that lived, you know, in places where-- mostly occupied by Gentiles.
But the majority was all-- we had our schools.
Ball, the-- not basket, no-- volleyball.
We played also with rocks, you know.
And, you know, the children-- mm-hmm.
There, there weren't that many children--
No, we bathed in the room.
And once a week, we were-- we were-- we're going to a public, public bathhouse once a week.
We had that little-- that little thing--
Well, we, we, we, we just-- it worked OK.
And we are off on a Saturday.
And Sunday, we were going to school.
Six days a week, yeah, at that time.
Polish history, yeah, a lot of Polish history.
It was only one class, religion.
So we-- there was one teacher that-- he taught us about the Bible, our-- and our religion.
But on the outskirts, I mean, you could have gone-- you know, mothers were sending children to special Jewish schools--
I mean, in a way-- they did in a way.
Because we were surrounded with everybody.
Like cooking-- my mother would cook for Passover and the holidays.
But my father wouldn't go down to a sukkah downstairs, you know.
--I questioned-- I always questioned them, why don't you do it?
Somehow, that's what happened, you know.
They, they-- he said-- my father used to say, what God wants from people is to be good, honest people.
And this-- the other is immaterial.
It could be that they would say a word or two.
--you know, very kind and good people, yeah.
I knew my father had four sisters, five-- or five sisters, mm-hmm.
They all were-- they all were there, mm-hmm, mm-hmm.
And they also came to the ghetto.
No, I mean before this-- before the war.
Yeah, we used to go and visit.
We used to go and visit--
Well, I wanted-- what did I want to be?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wanted to go to school, you know.
But I know we couldn't afford.
And to, to-- I liked to type, you know.
I-- I thought, I'll be a typist--
That's what I-- so in Germany, that's what I did.
I went to school and learned how to type.
After the war, yeah, yeah, mm-hmm.
The paper was every day in our house, the newspaper, the
And we knew what's going on with the German Jews.
And then we had neighbors that came-- they were thrown out from Germany.
And in 1938, the-- another family of theirs came.
Also they were, you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] emigrate-- not emigrated, but they were-- they were thrown out of--
Let's put it this way, yeah.
My parents, they were always together, just not to leave family, very knit-- close.
My sister wanted to go to, to Russia, I mean, when the war broke out--
She was already sitting with her luggage because she knew a young man that went to Russia-- Bialystok.
And he came back to bring her-- his parents back to Russia and promised her that she's going to go with him.
She was so anxious to go, you know.
She wanted to be in Russia, mostly, yeah, because-- away, away from--
And, and he was postponing, postponing until the ghetto was closed.
And nobody could go out anymore.
And she, she couldn't make it.
He wanted to be with his mother, or whoever.
And the tragic thing happened to him, tragic.
He, he was a big man-- very intelligent, big, big man.
I remember before the war, he used to come to my house.
He would eat up a whole bread that was two kilo.
He could eat it one time.
And of course, in, in, in the ghetto, he was so hungry.
So what he did, he saw a girl walking in the street with a bread.
And he had a stick and hit her over the head.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they, they hung him in the ghetto, this guy.
Then the, the Gestapo found him, mm-hmm.
And when my sister died, I-- he worked on the cemetery.
And I told him that she had passed away, you know.
The first memory-- we were told that the Germans are coming.
Everybody came down to that-- what do you call this, [NON-ENGLISH]?
This-- to that gate, you would come in to--
Oh, I know what you're saying, right.
And we all were there, because it was covered.
So if something happens, or-- or that they shoot-- or bombs or something, we will be more protected there.
And everybody was fearful and scared and, and frightened.
And we were waiting and waiting, sitting there, until someone came and said, no, they are not coming, you know, today.
The Germans are not coming today.
And you can go back to your rooms, to your houses.
And the next day, the Germans came in.
You saw 'em marching into the town.
We saw them marching in, with the-- with the, the tanks, and whatever.
And, you know-- and it was a very frightening thing to see them.
Oh, oh, no, what-- no, what happened-- what happened yet is the next day, they-- somebody came out with a story that all the men will be killed by the Germans if they're going to stay in Lodz.
So people took off like crazy to run.
And one of them was my father.
He came to Warsaw, you know.
Walked to Warsaw from Lodz, and-- with other people, of course.
And, and he went through all the horrible times there.
Bombs were falling over their heads, you know.
And and they said later it was just a propaganda.
They wanted the people to panic--
--and not know what to do, you know.
And a lot of them were killed on the road.
Anyway, a month later, he came back, walking, walking back again--
And he told us his-- you know, what that life was like there.
And, and, and slowly, slowly, you know, more and more food was taken away, you know.
The-- there were-- no, there weren't rations at that time yet.
But not so much food would come in.
In my-- in my-- where-- I, I was sheltered in my-- in my house.
And then we heard from others.
They were grabbing people in the streets.
And-- but they were grabbing the people to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there-- see, people were digging-- what do you call?
So the people were grabbed to cover them.
They took-- they took the people right away to hard work--
And some were killed right away.
Well, it was-- a nice lady just took him in.
And she was-- a family-- a family.
And they felt sorry for him to be all alone.
And he-- you know, he said how many people were killed.
The bombs were flying from all--
And I don't remember that much--
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then every day you had another announcement by the Germans, new laws, new laws.
Jews are supposed not to go here, not to go there.
Then the Jews have to wear the armband, you know.
And then-- and then they were throwing people out of their homes--
--the rich ones, taking everything away from them.
They went to the more expensive neighborhoods--
And we all have to leave to leave the, the houses and move into the ghetto.
Yeah, and leaflets would come in--
No, I think we bought them.
I think you, you bought them, yeah.
On our coats, yeah, whatever we were wearing, we had to-- we could-- we could not go out without them.
How difficult-- we had to find a little cart, a pushcart, you know, to put on all our belongings.
[LAUGHS] You know, and we put them on.
And, and as I told you, my father tried to go into business himself.
--to, to, to make a better living for his family.
And those machines meant, like-- you know, that he cherished them.
And this had to be put on-- also on that cart--
Whatever we could, we, we took with us.
And we were given a one-room apartment again, one room--
There-- it was that, that, that committee.
And-- yeah, because I think the Germans were in touch with the Jewish committee, so-- through them.
It was a smaller one, yeah.
And it's there where my grandmother passed away, you know.
I mean, there was still food.
We were complaining that this is not good and that is not good.
But there was still some food.
And systematically, it came to nothing.
--it, it went-- it went systematically, so we, we got used to it.
So for some people, they could survive.
In the ghetto-- we're in the ghetto, we got cards.
Committee would assign-- they were assigned, you know.
Yeah, the-- I don't remember who brought it in, but I know through--
My mother was doing all that, you know.
So I, I am not so familiar with.
I know there was the card, you know, that we could get a bread or half a bread or, or some potatoes or horse meat.
At that time-- was still horse meat.
And later on, there was no horse meat.
Now, who was going to eat potato peelings?
But later on, we're waiting for the potato peelings.
